subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the number of half lives covered by the radioactive material is given by

n=tT12   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the number of half lives covered.

n=15 years5 years= 3

The relation between the amount of radioactive substance after certain time can be written as

N=N02-n   ...2

Substitute the value of n into equation (2) to obtain the fraction of the remaining substance.

N=N02-3⇒NN0=18

Hence, the amount of the decayed substance can be written as

1-NN0=1-18=78

subject: Physics
options: neutron is a composite particle made of a proton and an electron
neutron has larger rest mass than proton
solution:
Mass of the neutron is mn=1.67493×10-27 kg,

mass of the proton is mp=1.67262×10-27 kg.

Proton's rest mass is less than that of a neutron's rest mass. As particle can not decay to a state of greater mass, hence the proton does not decay to neutron outside nucleus whereas the neutron can decay into a proton though β-decay.

subject: Physics
solution: After decay, the daughter nuclei will be more stable hence binding energy per nucleon will be more than that of their parent nucleus.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of radioactive decay law,

-dNdt=λ1N+λ2N  ...(1)

-dNdt=λ1+λ2N  ...(2)

Where,

-dNdt is the rate of decay

λ1, λ2 are decay constant for two different processes

N is the number of atoms.

Now applying the decay law for the combine process, we get

-dNdt=λeffN  ...(3)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get

λeff=λ1+λ2  ...(4)

Now the half life of the radioactive nucleus is given by,

λ=ln2T  ...(5)

Where, T is the half life of the radioactive nucleus

Using equation (5) equation (4) becomes,

ln2T=ln2T1+ln2T2

1T=1T1+1T2

1T=110+1100=11100

⇒T=10011≈9 s

subject: Physics
solution:

C136+Energy→C126+n10

The mass defect for the above reaction can be calculated as follows:

Δm=(12.000000+1.008665)-13.003354 u=-0.00531 u

Hence, the energy required is given by

E=0.00531×931.5 MeV=4.95 MeV

subject: Physics
solution:
${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$
power generated $=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{Q}$
where, $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow$ No. of reaction $/ \mathrm{sec}$.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\left(3 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) \mathrm{C}^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=(3 \times 4.0026-12)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=7.266 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\text { power }}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{5.808 \times 10^{30}}{7.266 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=5 \times 10^{42}\end{aligned}$
rate of conversion of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=15 \times 10^{42}$ Hence, $\mathrm{n}=15$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, for ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&A_0=16 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{~A}=12 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5760 \text { years }
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\lambda=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}$
$\lambda=\frac{0.693}{5760}$ per year
Then, from, $\mathrm{t}=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{A}_0}{\mathrm{~A}}$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} \frac{16}{12}
$$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} 1.333
$$
$=\frac{2.303 \times 5760 \times 0.1249}{0.693}$
$=2390.81 \approx 2391$ years

subject: Physics
solution:
Energy of γ ray Eγ=hv

Momentum of γ ray Pγ=hλ=hvc
Total momentum is conserved.

P→γ+P→Nu=0
where P→Nu= momentum of decayed nuclei

⇒Pγ=PNu
⇒hvc=PNu
⇒KE of nuclei

=12Mv2=PNu22M=12Mhvc2
Loss in internal energy =Eγ+KENu
=hv+12Mhvc2
=hv1+hv2Mc2

subject: Physics
solution:
For all nuclear processes, charge must be conserved. Also, when a release of an electron ( $e^{-}$) is always accompanied by a release of an antineutrino ( $\bar{v}$ )

Hence, $n \rightarrow p+\mathrm{e}^{-}+\overline{\mathrm{v}}$ is the correct answer.

subject: Physics
solution:
Binding energy is given by E=∆mc2

where ∆m is the mass defect.

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus having mass number 242 is 7.6 MeV.

The initial binding energy is,

BE=242×7.6 MeV

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus with mass number 121 is 8.1 MeV.

Therefore, binding energy is

BE'=2121×8.1 MeV

The gain in the binding energy is 

BE'-BE = (8.1 – 7.6) × 242 MeV=121 MeV

= 121 MeV

\end{aligned}$

⇒λeq=λ1+λ2

subject: Physics
solution:
Let N A and N B be the number of molecules of A and B after time t.

Also, after time t, NBNA=0.3(given).

Also, let N 0 be the total number of nucleus initial

After time t, N A + N B = N 0

N A +0.3 N A = N 0

∴   N A = N 0 1.3

Also, the rate of disintegration of A

N A = N 0 e −λt

⇒ N 0 1.3 = N 0 e −λt ;  1 1.3 = e −λt    or  log( 1.3 )=λt

t=log(1.3)λ

∴t=Tlog(1.3)log(2)=Tlog(1.3)log2.
